title: Fermat's Last Theorem for Selmer sections

		is bijective.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Fundamental anabelian conjecture linking arithmetic fundamental groups to rational points/cusps on hyperbolic curves; a major, deep open problem whose resolution would have transformative impact on Diophantine and anabelian geometry.
viability_rationale: Author claims a proof for ℓ≥7 using Serre's Frey-curve/modularity strategy together with Khare–Wintenberger. Method is classical and plausible, so likely resolved soon pending peer review and technical verification.

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex: If $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices, then $b(G) \le \lceil \sqrt{n}\rceil$.

interestingness_rationale: Central long-standing conjecture, sharp for paths and verified for many classes; recently proved asymptotically by Norin–Turcotte. Deep combinatorial and algorithmic implications (computing b(G) is NP-complete), so resolution would be significant.
viability_rationale: Norin–Turcotte (2024) establishes the BNC asymptotically and many graph classes are settled; strong partial progress and tree reduction make a resolution plausible, but tightening to exact ceil(√n) remains nontrivial.
rationale: The Burning Number Conjecture (BNC) is a well-known, actively posed open problem in graph theory referenced as a central open problem in the paper.

title: Weakening the Legendre Conjecture
body_tex:
There is a prime between $n^2$ and $(n + 1)^2$ for all positive integers

interestingness_rationale: Classic Landau problem; longstanding open question about prime gaps between consecutive squares, connected to RH and deep prime-distribution results. Resolving it would be a major advance in analytic number theory.
viability_rationale: Legendre is a century-old, famously hard problem; only RH-conditional progress (δ≥1/4 and large-x bounds) is reported. No unconditional breakthrough evidence — near-term full resolution unlikely.
rationale: Classical Legendre conjecture (prime between consecutive squares) is a long-standing open problem; paper states it explicitly as a conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture [Legendre]: There is a prime between n^2 and (n+1)^2 for all positive integers n. (lines 72–75)

title: A discrete view of Gromov's filling area conjecture
body_tex:
\label[conj]{gromovConjecture}
    If $M$ is a compact, orientable Riemannian surface which isometrically fills the Riemannian circle of length $2\pi$, then the surface area of $M$ is at least $2\pi$.
plain_text: If $M$ is a compact, orientable Riemannian surface which isometrically fills the Riemannian circle of length $2$, then the surface area of $M$ is at least $2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classic major open problem in Riemannian/filling geometry; proven only for disks and genus‑1, with only recent quantitative lower bounds (this paper: 1.36π). A full resolution would be deep and highly significant.
viability_rationale: Decades-old Gromov conjecture; only disk/genus-1 cases known. New discrete lower bound (≈1.36π) is modest progress but far from the 2π target, so full resolution in five years is unlikely.
rationale: Gromov's filling area conjecture is a classical, actively open conjecture (noted unresolved in the paper).
evidence_snippet: Lines 75–83 and 95–97: 'Gromov's filling area conjecture ... the full conjecture remains unresolved. \begin{conj}[Gromov's filling area conjecture] ... surface area ... at least $2\pi$.'

title: $k$-Positivity and high-dimensional bound entanglement under symplectic group symmetry
body_tex: [\cite{PPTsq, CMHW19}] \label{conj-PPT1}The composition of any two \emph{PPT} linear maps is \emph{entanglement breaking}. In other words, if $\Le_1$ and $\Le_2$ are CP linear maps whose Choi matrices are PPT, then the Choi matrix of $\Le_1\circ \Le_2$ is separable.
plain_text: [] The composition of any two PPT linear maps is entanglement breaking. In other words, if $_1$ and $_2$ are CP linear maps whose Choi matrices are PPT, then the Choi matrix of $_1 _2$ is separable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central open problem in quantum information/operator algebras; resolved only for d=2,3 with many partial results. A proof would clarify fundamental structure of PPT maps, entanglement breaking channels, and impact quantum repeater theory.
viability_rationale: Central, long-standing PPT-squared problem: only settled in d=2,3 and for restricted symmetric families (as here). Many partial results exist but no clear route to a full general proof — unlikely within five years.
rationale: This is the well-known PPT-squared conjecture in quantum information theory (composition of PPT maps is entanglement-breaking); actively open in general and cited as such.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1103–1106: Conjecture: The composition of any two PPT linear maps is entanglement breaking (if Le1,Le2 have PPT Choi matrices, Le1∘Le2 is separable).

viability_rationale: Widely known PPT-squared conjecture solved only in low dimensions; current progress is for special symmetric families (e.g., symplectic covariance). No general breakthrough indicated, so near-term resolution is unlikely.

title: Bounded gaps and perfect power gaps in sequences of consecutive primes
body_tex:
\lb{conj01}
  If the set $\{g_1x+h_1,g_2x+h_2,\dots g_kx+h_k\}\subseteq \mathbb Z[x]$
  is admissible, then there are infinitely many positive integers $n$
  for which every element of the set $\{g_1n+h_1,g_2n+h_2,\dots g_kn+h_k\}$
  is prime.
plain_text: conj01 If the set $\ g_1x+h_1,g_2x+h_2, g_kx+h_k\ Z[x]$ is admissible, then there are infinitely many positive integers $n$ for which every element of the set $\ g_1n+h_1,g_2n+h_2, g_kn+h_k\ $ is prime.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Deep generalization of prime k-tuples (implies twin primes etc.); central open problem whose resolution would settle many classical prime-distribution conjectures; only partial progress via Maynard–Tao bounded gaps.
viability_rationale: Dickson's conjecture is a decades-old grand problem; Maynard–Tao gives significant bounded-gap partial results but falls short of producing full prime k-tuples. A decisive resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: Dickson's conjecture is a classical, well-known open conjecture in number theory; the paper explicitly says it remains unsolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 626–633: Statement of Dickson's conjecture. Line 633–634: “Dickson's conjecture remains unsolved; however, …”

title: Decidability of Interpretability

interestingness_rationale: Central open problem in infinite-domain CSPs; generalizes Bulatov–Zhuk finite-domain dichotomy; deep model-theoretic and algebraic content via polymorphism clones; many special cases solved but full classification remains widely open and impactful.
viability_rationale: Many important special cases have been settled (Q,<; equality; poset; random graph; tournament; MMSNP), but the Bodirsky–Pinsker dichotomy remains wide-open in general; broad scope makes a near-term full resolution unlikely.
rationale: The Bodirsky–Pinsker conjecture is a well-known, widely stated open conjecture about P vs NP-complete dichotomy for CSPs of finitely bounded homogeneous reducts.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Bodirsky and Pinsker): Let C be a first-order reduct of a finitely bounded homogeneous structure A. Then CSP(C) is either in P or NP-complete. The conjecture is still wide-open in general.

title: On the list version of a conjecture of Erdős and Neumann-Lara

viability_rationale: Decades-old Erdős–Neumann–Lara conjecture (1979). Only fractional and list variants recently settled; original remains wide-open (f(3) even unclear). Little evidence of an imminent decisive breakthrough.

title: The circle method and pointwise ergodic theorems
body_tex:
Let $d, k, m\in\mathbb N$ be given and suppose that a family of invertible
measure-preserving transformations $T_1,\ldots, T_d:X\to X$ of a
probability measure space $(X, \mathcal B(X), \mu)$ generates a
nilpotent group. Assume that
$P_{1, 1},\ldots,P_{i, j},\ldots, P_{d, m}\in \mathbb Z[\mathrm n_1,\ldots, \mathrm n_k]$
are $k$-variate polynomials with integer coefficients. Then for any functions
$f_1, \ldots, f_m\in L^{\infty}(X)$, the non-conventional multilinear
polynomial averages
\begin{align}
\label{eq:29}
A_{N_1,\ldots, N_k; X,  T_1,\ldots, T_d}^{P_{1, 1}, \ldots, P_{d, m}}(f_1,\ldots, f_m)(x)=\mathbb E_{n\in[N_1]\times\ldots\times [N_k]}\prod_{j=1}^mf_j(T_1^{P_{1, j}(n)}\cdots T_d^{P_{d, j}(n)} x)
\end{align}
converge for $\mu$-almost every $x\in X$ as
$\min\{N_1,\ldots, N_k\}\to\infty$.  If $N_1=\ldots=N_k=N$ we shall
abbreviate
$A_{N_1,\ldots, N_k;X, T_1,\ldots, T_d}^{P_{1, 1}, \ldots, P_{d, m}}$
to $A_{N; X, T_1,\ldots, T_d}^{P_{1, 1}, \ldots, P_{d, m}}$.
plain_text: Let $d, k, m N$ be given and suppose that a family of invertible measure-preserving transformations $T_1,, T_d:X X$ of a probability measure space $(X, B(X), )$ generates a nilpotent group. Assume that $P_ 1, 1 ,,P_ i, j ,, P_ d, m Z[ n_1,, n_k]$ are $k$-variate polynomials with integer coefficients. Then for any functions $f_1, , f_m L^ (X)$, the non-conventional multilinear polynomial averages align A_ N_1,, N_k; X, T_1,, T_d ^ P_ 1, 1 , , P_ d, m (f_1,, f_m)(x)= E_ n [N_k] _ j=1 ^mf_j(T_1^ P_ 1, j (n) T_d^ P_ d, j (n) x) align converge for $$-almost every $x X$ as $\ N_1,, N_k\ $. If $N_1==N_k=N$ we shall abbreviate $A_ N_1,, N_k;X, T_1,, T_d ^ P_ 1, 1 , , P_ d, m $ to $A_ N; X, T_1,, T_d ^ P_ 1, 1 , , P_ d, m $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central, widely recognized open problem generalizing polynomial nonconventional ergodic averages for nilpotent actions; flagged as a major challenge linking pointwise ergodic theory and modern Fourier analysis, with high impact if resolved.
viability_rationale: Classic longstanding Furstenberg–Bergelson–Leibman conjecture; described as a major open problem with only partial progress in pointwise ergodic theory. No local indication of an imminent full resolution in five years.

title: Dead ends in square-free digit walks
body_tex:
There exists an infinite sequence of square-free integers
\[ \{N_0,N_1,N_2, N_3, \dots\} \]
and digits
$d_{k+1}\in\{0,1,\dots,9\}$ such that $N_{k+1}=10N_k+d_{k+1}$ for all $k\ge 0$.
plain_text: There exists an infinite sequence of square-free integers \[ \ N_0,N_1,N_2, N_3, \ \] and digits $d_ k+1 \ 0,1,,9\ $ such that $N_ k+1 =10N_k+d_ k+1 $ for all $k 0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural open problem: probabilistic models predict infinite walks, but dependencies block proof. Connects to squarefree distribution, branching processes, and explicit infinite constructions—important and likely deep.
viability_rationale: Heuristic branching-process predicts survival (~0.99991), but strong dependencies (mod p^2 constraints) and no explicit construction or control of correlations make a rigorous existence proof hard; modest short-term chance of breakthrough.
rationale: The Miller et al. conjecture about infinite square-free digit walks is explicitly stated and the authors note it remains open; no proof is given.
evidence_snippet: "Despite its innocent appearance, this conjecture remains open." "no one has exhibited an explicit infinite sequence ... nor has the existence ... been established."

title: Almost all primes are partially regular
body_tex:

plain_text: If $p$ is an odd prime, then we have \[ A_p(^ 2k ) = 0 for all even 2k \ 2, 4, , p-3\ . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classical Vandiver conjecture: asserts vanishing of plus-eigenspaces of p-class group for all p; central, longstanding open problem linking Bernoulli numbers, cyclotomic class groups, Iwasawa theory; resolution would be major advance.
viability_rationale: The paper already proves a Corollary from Theorem T2: for α>1/2 almost all primes have initial even eigenspaces trivial up to M_α(p). Strong partial results and classical links (Herbrand–Ribet) make near-term resolution plausible.
rationale: This is the classical Kummer–Vandiver (Vandiver) conjecture: a long-standing, actively posed open problem asserting vanishing of the plus-eigenspaces; it remains unresolved in current number theory literature.
evidence_snippet: “the ‘plus’ part A_p^+ is the subject of Vandiver's Conjecture… If p is an odd prime, then we have A_p(ω^{2k}) = 0 for all even 2k in {2,4,…,p-3}.”

title: Multivariate and quantitative Erdős-Kac laws for Beatty sequences
plain_text: Let $f$ be a polynomial with at least one irrational non-constant coefficient. For $n U[N]$, the random variable equation* (f(n)) - N N equation* converges in distribution to $N(0, 1)$ as $N$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalizes Erdős–Kac to generalized polynomials; unifies Beatty and integer-polynomial cases, unresolved beyond finite-type leading coefficients; solving would significantly advance understanding of prime factor distribution in nonlinear sequences.
viability_rationale: Many cases already resolved: Weyl+Fourier handle finite-type/algebraic leading coefficients and prior multivariate results cover Diophantine parameters. Only pathological Liouville-type exceptions remain; active partial progress yields modest near-term hope.
rationale: The author formulates Conjecture (gen poly conj) on Erdős–Kac for floor(f(n)) with irrational coefficient, and discusses partial results, so it is an open research conjecture.
evidence_snippet: “\begin{conj} \label{gen poly conj} Let f be a polynomial with at least one irrational non-constant coefficient… converges to N(0,1).” (lines 147–153).

title: Burning rooted graph products

viability_rationale: Well-known open burning-number conjecture reduced to trees; problem remains NP-complete for trees and snippet shows no decisive breakthroughs. Partial/structural progress but low chance of resolution within five years.

title: Spectral boundaries of deterministic matrices deformed by rotationally invariant random non-Hermitian ensembles
body_tex:
\label{main_conjecture}
Let $\vb{A}$ be a large deterministic matrix and $\vb{B}$ a rotationally invariant random matrix. Then, for large $\omega$ and $z\in\C$, one expects
\begin{equation}\label{eq_conjecture}
    \mathcal{G}_{\vb{A} + \vb{B}}(\omega, z)
    \;=\; \mathcal{G}_{\vb{A}}\!\left(\omega - \mathcal{R}_{1, \vb{B}}(\g_1,\g_2),\, z - \mathcal{R}_{2, \vb{B}}(\g_1,\g_2)\right)
\end{equation}where $\g_1 = \g_{1, \vb{A} + \vb{B}}$ and $\g_2 = \g_{2, \vb{A} + \vb{B}}$. 
This identity can be analytically continued to all $(\omega,z)$, although one must carefully choose the appropriate branches of $\mathcal{R}_1$ and $\mathcal{R}_2$.
plain_text: Let $A$ be a large deterministic matrix and $B$ a rotationally invariant random matrix. Then, for large $$ and $z$, one expects equation G_ A + B (, z) \;=\; G_ A \!( - R_ 1, B (_1,_2),\, z - R_ 2, B (_1,_2)) equationwhere $_1 = _ 1, A + B $ and $_2 = _ 2, A + B $. This identity can be analytically continued to all $(,z)$, although one must carefully choose the appropriate branches of $R_1$ and $R_2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalizes additive subordination/R-transform to non‑Hermitian rotationally invariant ensembles; proving it would give simple spectral-edge equations and deeply advance non‑Hermitian free probability—technical and impactful.
viability_rationale: Not a decades-old grand problem and restricted to rotationally invariant ensembles (helps), but requires delicate non-Hermitian subordination/analytic continuation; no clear decisive progress shown, so modest near-term chance.
rationale: Explicitly stated as a main conjecture and used as an assumption to prove results; presented as open (not proven) in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 764–771: "\begin{conjecture}... Then, for large ... one expects ... This identity can be analytically continued ... \end{conjecture}. We now aim to prove Theorem by relying on the conjecture."

viability_rationale: Proved for orders ≤7 with further supporting evidence; method may extend but complexity and parity issues grow with n. Tied to broader unresolved Ustinov finite-rank questions, so general resolution within five years is plausible but uncertain.
rationale: The paper explicitly states Conjecture (gre) about diamond rank bounds for even-order Gale-Robinson sequences; it's presented as an open generalisation supported by experiments.

viability_rationale: Current constructions yield uniform gaps ~0.82 and arithmetic families with gaps well below 2; authors give no route to >2. Floer-theoretic constraints and related obstructions make a decisive resolution within five years unlikely.

title: Classification of closed minimal hypersurfaces with constant scalar curvature in $\mathbb{S}^5$

    \textup{(Chern's Conjecture)} Let \(M^{n}\) be a closed immersed minimal hypersurface of the unit sphere \(\mathbb{S}^{n+1}\) with constant scalar curvature. Then for each \(n\), the set of all possible values for $S$ should be discrete.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Long-standing, famous Chern conjecture (listed by Yau); central to classification of minimal hypersurfaces in spheres. Partial gap results and n=3 solution exist, but the general case remains deep and influential.
viability_rationale: Chern's conjecture is a long-standing, difficult problem, but the S^5 (n=4) low-dimensional special case has active partial results and targeted progress, giving a modest near-term chance of resolution.
rationale: Chern's conjecture is a classical, still-open problem in differential geometry (proved in special cases like n=3); the paper cites it as an open conjecture motivating results.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 88–91): "(Chern's Conjecture) ... the set of all possible values for S should be discrete." Paper notes it remains an important unsolved problem.

title: Surface topology and incidence theorems over division rings
body_tex: Let $\mathcal T$ be a simple, $(n+1)$-degenerate tiling of a surface of positive genus, and let $\D$ be a division ring. Then the incidence theorem corresponding to $\mathcal T$ holds in $\DP^n$ if and only if $\D$ is commutative.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalizes Pappus-type equivalence between commutativity and incidence theorems to higher genus and dimension; links topology, combinatorics, and noncommutative projective geometry. Solving it would significantly advance understanding—likely difficult.
viability_rationale: Recent partial results and an inductive 3-degenerate construction suggest feasibility for many genus>0 tilings; but broad quantification over all (n+1)-degenerate tilings and absence of a general proof make near-term resolution uncertain.
rationale: Paper explicitly states this incidence/degeneracy conjecture for tilings on positive-genus surfaces; presented as a conjecture and not proved here.
evidence_snippet: “Conjecture: Let T be a simple, (n+1)-degenerate tiling of a surface of positive genus, and let D be a division ring. Then the incidence theorem ... holds in DP^n iff D is commutative.”

title: Surface topology and incidence theorems over division rings
body_tex:
For any $g > 0$, there exists a constant $n_g$ such that, for any simple tiling $\mathcal T$ of a genus $g$ surface, the corresponding  incidence theorem over a division ring $\D$ is equivalent to commutativity of $\D$
in any dimension $n \geq n_g$. In particular, one has $n_1 = 3$, so that any theorem in dimension $n \geq 3$ corresponding to a tiling of the torus holds over a division ring $\D$ if and only if $\D$ is commutative.
plain_text: For any $g > 0$, there exists a constant $n_g$ such that, for any simple tiling $ T$ of a genus $g$ surface, the corresponding incidence theorem over a division ring $$ is equivalent to commutativity of $$ in any dimension $n n_g$. In particular, one has $n_1 = 3$, so that any theorem in dimension $n 3$ corresponding to a tiling of the torus holds over a division ring $$ if and only if $$ is commutative.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects surface genus and graph degeneracy to algebraic commutativity (e.g. n_1=3 for the torus), extending the Pappus/Desargues dichotomy; requires deep synthesis of combinatorial topology and noncommutative projective geometry.
viability_rationale: Partial results and constructive 3‑degenerate method (Pappus example) and genus‑degeneracy bounds provide tangible routes; but the conjecture is broad across surfaces/dimensions and hinges on delicate noncommutative obstructions.
rationale: Authors pose the existence of n_g for genus g as a conjecture linking tilings on genus-g surfaces to commutativity of division rings; stated explicitly and treated as open.
evidence_snippet: “Conjecture: For any g>0, there exists n_g such that for any simple tiling of a genus g surface, the corresponding incidence theorem over a division ring D is equivalent to commutativity of D in any dimension n ≥ n_g.”

  Then $q_{n + 1} < (q_n + 1)^2$ for all $n$.

title: Concentration for random Euclidean combinatorial optimization
body_tex:
\label{conj:ptoq}

$p$--optimal matching $\sigma_p$,
\[
\EE\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n |X_i-Y_{\sigma_p(i)}|^{q}\Big]
\;\le\; c\, n^{1-q/d}.
\]

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: If true, yields universal q-moment bounds matching the n^{-1/d} matching scale, removing the p<d^2/2 restriction and extending concentration results; proving optimizer-edge tail control is technically deep.
viability_rationale: Authors obtain sup-edge bounds and close Poincaré estimate for p<d^2/2, with numerical support; conjecture needs stronger control of higher moments—plausible with current partial progress but requires new technical advances.
rationale: Stated explicitly as a conjectural p→q transfer principle; authors note its truth would extend their results, indicating it is open.
evidence_snippet: "For every q>p there exists c ... such that for every p-optimal matching \sigma_p, E[\sum |X_i-Y_{\sigma_p(i)}|^q] \le c n^{1-q/d}." (Conjecture)

title: Universal Functions for Topological Correlators
body_tex:
\label{conj_GK}
Let $M$ be as above, and $s\in\mathbb Z$. There exist universal functions
\bea\label{Cz Cz12}
Q_s, R_s, T_s, V_s, W_s, X_s  &\in \mathbb C[[z]]~, \\
S_s, S_{1,s}, Y_s, Y_{1,s}, Z_s,  Z_{11,s} &\in \mathbb C[[z^{\frac{1}{2}}]]~,
\eea
such that for any $\alpha\in K(S)$ with $\rk(\alpha)=s$ we have 
{\normalfont
\bea\label{GK conjecture}
\segre_\alpha[p',x']=\universal_\alpha[p',x'] \Big|_{z^{\frac12\vd M}}~,
\eea
}
where 
{\normalfont
\bea\label{universal series}
\universal_\alpha[p',x']& \coloneqq 2^{2-\chih+K^2}V_s^{c_2(\alpha)}W_s^{c_1(\alpha)^2}X_s^{\chih} Y_s^{c_1(\alpha)K}Z_s^{K^2}e^{x'^2 Q_s + c_1(\alpha)x' R_s + x'K S_{s}  +  p'\, T_s} \\
&\quad \times \sum_a (-1)^{a c_1}\, \sw(a) Y_{1,s}^{c_1(\alpha)a}e^{a x' S_{1,s}} Z_{11,s}^{a^2}~,
\eea
}
and 
\bea\label{changeofvar}
z = t (1+(1-\tfrac{s}{2}) t)^{1-\frac{s}{2}}~.
\eea
Here, 
\bea\label{Vs Ws Xs Qs Rs Ts} 
V_s(t) &= (1+(1-\tfrac{s}{2})t)^{2-s} (1+(2-\tfrac{s}{2})t)^s~, \\
W_{s}(t)&= 2\left(2+(2-s)t\right)^{-\frac32+\frac12s}\left(2+(4-s)t\right)^{\frac12-\frac12s}~, \\
X_{s}(t)&= (1+(1-\tfrac{s}{2})t)^{\frac12s^2-\frac54s}(1+(2-\tfrac{s}{2})t)^{-\frac12 s^2+\frac12}(1+(1-\tfrac{s}{2})(2-\tfrac{s}{2})t)^{-\frac12}~, \\
Q_s(t) &= \tfrac{1}{2}t(1+(1-\tfrac{s}{2}) t)~, \quad  R_s(t) = t~, \quad 
T_{s}(t) = 2 t (1+ \tfrac{1}{2}(1-\tfrac{s}{2})(2-\tfrac{s}{2})t)~, \\
\eea
and moreover $Y_s$, $Y_{1,s}$,  $Z_s$, $Z_{11,s}$, $S_s$, $S_{1,s}$ are all algebraic functions.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes universal algebraic generating functions linking Segre numbers, moduli of sheaves and Seiberg–Witten/Donaldson invariants; proving it would significantly unify enumerative algebraic geometry and gauge-theory invariants.
viability_rationale: Recent explicit formulas and verifications for s=1,2,3 and modular/algebraic structure (Göttsche–Kool 2020) indicate active progress, but full rank-2 universality remains nontrivial, so only a moderate near-term chance.
rationale: This is the Göttsche–Kool conjecture (cited explicitly) about universal functions for Segre invariants; presented as a conjecture in the literature and in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1045–1053 and 1060–1067: "Before we state the main conjecture..." then Conjecture (Göttsche--Kool) with universal functions and formulas.

title: On the Coupled Cluster Doubles Truncation Variety of Four Electrons
body_tex:
Consider the rational map $\gamma: \mathbb P^m \dashrightarrow \mathbb P^{m+s}$ such that 
\[ \begin{matrix}
\gamma: & \mathbb P^m \setminus \textrm{V}(z_0) & \longrightarrow & \mathbb P^{m+s}\\
& z & \mapsto & y 
\end{matrix} \quad \text{with} \quad y_i = \begin{cases}
    z_0^2 & \text{if }i=0,\\
    z_0z_i & \text{if } i \in \{1,\ldots,m\},\\
    \gamma_i(z) & \text{if } i \in \{ m+1, \ldots, m+s\},
\end{cases}
\]
where $\gamma_i(z)$ are general homogeneous quadratic polynomials for $i=m+1,\ldots, m+s$. Then the graph of $\gamma$ has degree
\[ \deg(\text{graph}(\gamma)) = \begin{cases}
(m - s + 2)2^s -1 & \text{if } s<m, \\
2^{m+1}-1 & \text{if } s \geq m.
\end{cases} \]

title: Minimal Inversions in Integer Matrices of Fixed RSK Shape
body_tex:
\label{conj:hankel}
Let $\lambda$ be a partition with $n$ parts, and let 
$M=(m_{i,j})_{1\le i,j \le n} \in \mathcal{M}_\lambda$ 
be a minimal matrix of shape $\lambda$. Then $M$ is symmetric, that is,
\[
m_{i,j} = m_{j,i} \quad \text{for all } 1 \le i,j \le n.
\]
Moreover, every minimal matrix of shape $\lambda$ is Hankel: there exists a sequence of integers 
\[
s_2, s_3, \dots, s_{2n}
\]
such that
\[
m_{i,j} = s_{i+j}
\quad \text{for all } 1 \le i,j \le n.
\]

title: Research Report: some constructions in Polyhedral Symplectic Topology suggested by AI
body_tex:
Let $\Gamma_{v,\delta} \subset (S^3,\xi_{\mathrm{std}})$ be the Legendrian link obtained by intersecting a locally smoothed valence--$4$ vertex with a sufficiently small sphere. Then the following hold:

\smallskip

\noindent
\textup{(i)} If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies 
\[
\mu(K,v)=0,
\]
then 
\[
\mathrm{rot}(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
tb(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)=-1.
\]
In particular, $\Gamma_v^\varepsilon$ is Legendrian isotopic to the standard Legendrian unknot.

\smallskip

\noindent
\textup{(ii)} If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies 
\[
\mu(K,v)\not=0,
\]
then 
\[
\mathrm{rot}(\Gamma_v^\varepsilon)\neq 0,
\]
and therefore $\Gamma_v^\varepsilon$ cannot bound a Lagrangian disk inside $B_\varepsilon(v)$. In particular, the vertex is not locally Lagrangian smoothable.

(ii) If the polyhedral Maslov index satisfies \[ (K,v)=0, \] then \[ rot(_v^) 0, \] and therefore $_v^$ cannot bound a Lagrangian disk inside $B_(v)$. title: Contention Resolution, With and Without a Global Clock
body_tex:
\label{conj:global-clock}
Consider the class of randomized acknowledgment-based protocols in the \GlobalClock{} model.
\begin{enumerate}
\item In the finite setting, there exists a 
        protocol with latency $O(n)$ with high probability against an adaptive adversary.
 
\item In the unbounded setting, there exists a 
protocol that utilizes the shared resource at some rate $\lambda$,\footnote{Here we have in mind the definition of utilization implicit in the analysis of~\cite{ChangJP19}.  Utilization $\lambda$ is achieved if (1) there is a potential function $\Phi$ such that waking up $n'$ parties increases $\Phi$ 
by at most $n'/\lambda$,  
(2) if $\Phi = \Omega(1)$ is sufficiently large, executing the protocol for another time step decreases $\Phi$ 
by $1+\epsilon$ in expectation, $\epsilon>0$, 
and (3) if $\Phi=O(1)$ is bounded, 
the number of 
still-unsuccessful parties left in the system is also $O(1)$.
This definition does not constrain the adversary, e.g., 
by limiting the wake-up rate to 
$\lambda$ over certain windows 
of time~\cite{BenderFHKL05}.} 
even against an adaptive adversary.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Consider the class of randomized acknowledgment-based protocols in the model. enumerate In the finite setting, there exists a protocol with latency $O(n)$ with high probability against an adaptive adversary. In the unbounded setting, there exists a protocol that utilizes the shared resource at some rate $$,Here we have in mind the definition of utilization implicit in the analysis of~. Utilization $$ is achieved if (1) there is a potential function $$ such that waking up $n'$ parties increases $$ by at most $n'/$, (2) if $ = (1)$ is sufficiently large, executing the protocol for another time step decreases $$ by $1+$ in expectation, $>0$, and (3) if $=O(1)$ is bounded, the number of still-unsuccessful parties left in the system is also $O(1)$. This definition does not constrain the adversary, e.g., by limiting the wake-up rate to $$ over certain windows of time~. even against an adaptive adversary. enumerate
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asks for O(n) latency w.h.p. and constant-rate utilization against adaptive adversaries in the GlobalClock model, improving n(loglog n)^{1+o(1)} bounds. Solving it would significantly advance contention-resolution theory and adversarial distributed algorithms.

title: Towards Lang--Vojta via Degeneration
body_tex: \label{conjecture:Lang--Vojta} Let $k$ be a number field and $X/k$ a smooth projective variety, with canonical sheaf $\mathcal{K}$. Let $D$ be an effective, reduced, normal crossings divisor on $X$ and let $S$ be any finite set of primes in $\mathcal{O}_k$. If $\mathcal{K}\otimes\mathcal{O}_X(D)$ is big, then any set of $(D,S)$-integral points in $X$ is Zariski degenerate (i.e. not  dense in the Zariski topology).

Let $D$ be an effective, reduced, normal crossings divisor on $X$ and let $S$ be any finite set of primes in $O_k$. If $K_X(D)$ is big, then any set of $(D,S)$-integral points in $X$ is Zariski degenerate (i.e. not dense in the Zariski topology).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central open problem in Diophantine geometry extending Bombieri–Lang and Vojta: predicts Zariski non-density of (D,S)-integral points for log-general-type pairs. Deep, longstanding, and broadly consequential if resolved.

title: Quantitative partitioned index theorem and noncompact band-width
body_tex:

Let $N$ be a compact manifold without boundary, of dimension $n-1 \geq 5$. Suppose that $N$ does not admit Riemannian metrics of positive scalar curvature. Consider a Riemannian metric $g$ on $N \times [-1,1]$. Let $\ell$ be the distance between $N \times \{-1\}$ and $N \times \{1\}$ with respect to this metric. Then
\[

\]

title: Tilted Richardson Varieties
body_tex:
For a tilted Bruhat interval $[u, v]$, define the \emph{tilted Bruhat interval graph} as the directed graph with vertex set $[u, v]$ and an edge $w \xrightarrow{e_i - e_j} wt_{ij}$ whenever $w \prec wt_{ij}$ in the poset $[u, v]$. Here, recall that $w$ and $wt_{ij}$ are always comparable by \Cref{cor:tilted-bruhat-graph}. For any reflection ordering $\gamma$ of the set of positive roots $\Phi^+$, we have
\[R^\tilt_{u,v}(q)=q^{\frac{\ell(u,v)}{2}}\sum_{P:u\to v}\left(q^{\frac{1}{2}}-q^{-\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{\ell(P)},\]
where the sum is over all directed paths $P$ from $u$ to $v$ in the tilted Bruhat interval graph whose sequence of edge labels is strictly increasing with respect to $\gamma$ and $\ell(P)$ denotes the number of edges in the path $P$.

title: On the expected value of energy in groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:Jeff}
Let $G$ be an infinite group and let $S$ be a finite generating set for $G$. Then, 
\begin{enumerate}[(i)]
\item $\iota_S(G)$ is positive if, and only if, $G$ is virtually abelian.
\item for every function $k \colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $k \ll \sqrt[4]{|{\bf B}_S(n)|}$, and for every $\A$, $k$-subset of $G$ sampled uniformly at random in $\B_S(n)$, we have 
$$\E[E(\A, \A^{-1})] = 2  k^2 - k \,. $$
\end{enumerate}

title: An elementary approach to non-symmetric shift operators and their q-analogs
body_tex:
\label{conj:fundamental_shift_operator}
    For any linear character $\eps$ of $W$, there exists a non-symmetric shift operator $\tilde{\calG}^{(\eps)}_\pm(k)$ with shift $\pm l$ that is fundamental in the following sense. All non-symmetric shift operators with shift $\pm l$ are of the form
    \[
        \tilde{\calG}^{(\eps)}_\pm(k)T_p(k) \qquad \text{for some } p \in S(\frakh).
    \]
    In particular, the non-symmetric shift operator $\calG^{(\eps)}_\pm(k)$ decomposes as follows:
    \[
        \calG^{(\eps)}_\pm(k) = \tilde{\calG}^{(\eps)}_\pm(k) \prod_{\alpha \in R^0_+, \thinspace l(\alpha) = 0}(T_{\alpha^\vee}(k) - k^0(\alpha)).
    \]
    Observe that $\calG^{(\eps)}_\pm(k) = \tilde{\calG}^{(\eps)}_\pm(k)$ for the sign character, so that the conjecture states that $\calG^{(\eps)}_\pm(k)$ is fundamental in this case.

title: Lower Bounds for the Pfaffian Number of Graphs
body_tex:
Let $G$ be a matchable graph.
	Let $H'$ be a non-spanning conformal subgraph of $G$ and let $H'' := G - V(H')$.
	If $H'$ and $H''$ are not pfaffian, then
		\[ \pf(H') + \pf(H'') - 1 < \pf(G).  \]

$$\eptrzf{(K_{a,b}, \{v\})} < \eptrzf{(K_{a,b}, \{u\})}$$

title: Rigidity of proper holomorphic maps between balls with Hölder boundary regularity
body_tex:
Suppose $f: \Bb^m \rightarrow \Bb^M$ is a proper holomorphic map which extends to H\"older continuous map $\overline{\Bb^m} \rightarrow \overline{\Bb^M}$. If the image of the natural projection $\mathsf{G}_f \rightarrow \Aut(\Bb^m)$ contains a uniform lattice, then there exist $\phi_1 \in \Aut(\Bb^m)$ and $\phi_2 \in \Aut(\Bb^M)$ such that 
$$
\phi_2 \circ f \circ \phi_1(z) = (z,0)
$$
for all $z \in \Bb^m$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Equivalent to a well-known rigidity conjecture in complex hyperbolic geometry; partial results (Cao–Mok, Yue) cover special ranges, but the full lattice-preserving totally geodesic embedding remains open and central to rigidity theory.

Let $\mathfrak g=\mathfrak{gl}(n|n)$, let $\mathfrak b$ be a Borel subalgebra, and let $\alpha$ be a

\[
\operatorname{DS}_{e_\alpha}\bigl(M^{\mathfrak b}(\lambda)\bigr)\ \cong\
\begin{cases}
M^{\mathfrak b_{e_\alpha}}\bigl(\operatorname{pr}_\alpha(\lambda)\bigr)\ \oplus\

& \text{if }(\lambda,\alpha)=0,\\[2mm]
0, & \text{if }(\lambda,\alpha)\neq 0.
\end{cases}
\]
Here $\operatorname{pr}_\alpha$ is the natural projection on weight lattices associated with $\alpha$, defined
in \S6.

title: Almost prime numbers
body_tex:
\label{ExistAlmostPrime}
Are there any composite weakly almost- primes greater than four or any composite almost-primes ?
plain_text: Are there any composite weakly almost- primes greater than four or any composite almost-primes ?
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Any almost-prime must be a Carmichael number; existence would impose strong uniform constraints on p-1 across prime divisors and impact infinitude questions about primes with ω(p-1)≤2.
viability_rationale: Specialized existence question; authors reduced almost-primes to Carmichael numbers and proved strong uniform divisibility constraints (2-adic, Fermat primes), creating tractable structure—moderate likelihood amenable to construction or elimination within five years.
rationale: Authors explicitly pose the existence question for composite weakly almost-primes/almost-primes as an open conjecture in the Introduction and investigate it throughout the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 69–72: 'Clearly any prime is an almost-prime... the following conjecture arises: Are there any composite weakly almost-primes greater than four or any composite almost-primes?'

title: Almost prime numbers
body_tex: There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers.
plain_text: There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Strengthening Alford–Granville–Pomerance by restricting Carmichael numbers to satisfy rigid congruence conditions; connects to primes with ω(p−1)≤2 and deep distributional questions—likely difficult and important to specialists.
viability_rationale: New, non-classic conjecture. Authors reduce to Carmichael numbers and prove strong algebraic congruence constraints but provide no constructions; restrictive conditions suggest difficulty. Moderate-low chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: Paper formulates the strengthened conjecture that infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers exist; presented as an open conjecture following prior results.
evidence_snippet: Lines 94–98: '...the following strengthened version arises naturally. Conjecture: There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers.'

viability_rationale: Classical Poincaré–Bertrand is established; authors provide analytic quasi-periodic Green's expansions and high-accuracy numerics. Standard Calderón/potential-theory techniques likely adapt to the quasi-periodic setting, so a proof seems plausible soon.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Positivity and palindromic symmetry of invariants would reveal deep structural constraints, underpinning finite-rank phenomena; supported by computations for orders ≤9 and connections to known Laurent-positivity results, but likely technically challenging to prove generally.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence for many cases (n≤25) and small-n proofs, but arguments become prohibitively difficult and new insights are required for infinite families; moderate chance of partial progress, low chance of full resolution.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture supplementing Conjecture \ref{cinv}, presented as an open experimental suggestion for higher orders.
evidence_snippet: The experimental data suggests the following supplements: \begin{conjecture} \label{cinve} ... \end{conjecture} (lines 529–531).

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{grte} Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then, generically, the matrix $s \times t$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then, generically, the matrix $s t$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends proven finite-rank phenomena (orders 6–7) to all even orders for Gale-Robinson/Somos sequences; resolving it would reveal deep structure in recurrences/cluster-algebra-related invariants and likely need new methods beyond current computational reach.
viability_rationale: True for small orders (n≤9) with extensive experiments to n≈25; patterns are concrete but require new theoretical insights beyond heavy computation. Plausible but uncertain to be resolved within five years.
rationale: Authors pose Conjecture \ref{grte} as an open generalisation about diamond rank for twinned Gale-Robinson sequences; supported by experiments but not proved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture \label{grte}: ... the matrix s × t is of diamond rank at most 2^m. (lines 537–541).

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{grto} Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then, generically, the matrix $s \times t$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then, generically, the matrix $s t$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalises finite‑rank phenomena for Somos/Gale‑Robinson sequences and ties to cluster‑algebra/Laurent and integrable‑systems structure. Supported by small‑n experiments but computationally intractable; likely deep and important to specialists if resolved.
viability_rationale: Strong experimental support up to order 9, but computations become prohibitive and no general method is proposed; broad infinite-family claims likely need new ideas, so near-term resolution is unlikely.
rationale: Conjecture \ref{grto} is explicitly stated as an open conjecture about half-diamond rank for odd order Gale-Robinson sequences.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture \label{grto}: ... the matrix s × t is of half-diamond rank at most 2^m. (lines 539–541).

interestingness_rationale: Natural generalization of Vergne’s filiform classification; proving it would structurally classify compatible filiform Lie algebras and significantly impact deformation/cohomology theory and solvable-extension constructions.
viability_rationale: Natural generalization of Vergne with concrete reductions: authors reduce to one filiform bracket plus a cocycle and develop graded/filiform machinery—likely approachable by extending Vergne-style deformation methods within a few years.

viability_rationale: Concrete finite statement for a specific filiform nilradical; authors built extensions up to +5 and note maximal torus =2. Structural derivation/torus methods likely settle this; plausible within a few years.

title: Variants of Merge-Width and Applications
body_tex:
\label{conj:univ}
Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded universal merge-width.
plain_text: Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded universal merge-width.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Would strengthen robustness of merge-width, connecting to bounded expansion, twin-width and FO-transductions; known in special cases, open generally; solution impacts structural theory and algorithmic consequences.
viability_rationale: Strong partial progress (bounded-expansion and twin-width cases, FO-transduction preservation, and quasi-isometry reduction) makes a resolution plausible, but no general proof or counterexample is yet evident.
rationale: Explicit conjecture in the merge-width paper: equivalence of bounded merge-width and bounded universal merge-width; authors give supporting evidence but no proof.
evidence_snippet: "We conjecture the following. Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded universal merge-width." (Followed by corroborating facts, no proof.)

title: Variants of Merge-Width and Applications
body_tex:
\label{conj:short}
Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded short merge-width.
plain_text: Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded short merge-width.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Links general merge-width to a length-restricted variant; a positive resolution yields adjacency-labeling and strong structural consequences. Only shown for bounded expansion and FO transductions, so the claim is nontrivial and likely deep.
viability_rationale: Several broad families (bounded expansion, FO transductions) are proved to have bounded short merge-width and technical reductions exist, but the general implication remains nontrivial; moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: Explicitly posed conjecture that bounded merge-width implies bounded short merge-width; presented as an open problem despite supportive partial results.
evidence_snippet: "All of this motivates us to pose the following. Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded short merge-width."

title: Colour diversity in spanning structures under Dirac-type conditions
body_tex: Let $1/2<c\le1$. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with $\delta(G)\ge cn$, then every proper edge-colouring of $G$ contains at least $n/8$ Hamilton cycles, each using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
plain_text: Let $1/2<c1$. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with $(G) cn$, then every proper edge-colouring of $G$ contains at least $n/8$ Hamilton cycles, each using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Strengthens the Dirac/Christofides–Kühn–Osthus n/8 edge-disjoint Hamilton cycle result by requiring each cycle to be nearly maximally rainbow under proper edge-colouring; merges extremal, decomposition and anti-Ramsey difficulties, so is technically deep and significant.
viability_rationale: Single-cycle coloured Dirac result proved; uncoloured n/8 packing is known. Extending packing/absorption to preserve colour diversity seems plausible but nontrivial, giving a moderate chance within five years.
rationale: Authors propose new conjectures in concluding remarks about many colourful Hamilton cycles and colourful tree embeddings; these are presented as open problems.
evidence_snippet: "Let 1/2 < c ≤ 1. If G ... δ(G) ≥ cn, then every proper edge-colouring of G contains at least n/8 Hamilton cycles, each using at least cn - o(n) distinct colours." (Conjecture in concluding remarks.)

title: Colour diversity in spanning structures under Dirac-type conditions
body_tex:
Let $1/2<c\le1$. Suppose that $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree
$\delta(G)\ge cn$. Then there exists a constant $d>0$ such that for every tree $T$ on $n$ vertices with $\Delta(T)\le dn/\log n$, any proper edge-colouring of $G$
contains a copy of $T$ using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
plain_text: Let $1/2<c1$. Suppose that $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $(G) cn$. Then there exists a constant $d>0$ such that for every tree $T$ on $n$ vertices with $(T) dn/ n$, any proper edge-colouring of $G$ contains a copy of $T$ using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural colourful analogue of the Komlós–Sárközy–Szemerédi tree-embedding under Dirac conditions; resolving it would significantly extend rainbow/Dirac theory and likely requires substantial new embedding and colouring techniques.
viability_rationale: Authors prove an almost-maximal colourful Hamilton cycle and an uncoloured n/8 edge-disjoint result exists; extending to many almost-rainbow cycles seems plausible but technically challenging, so moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Explicitly posed in the concluding remarks as a new colourful analogue and not resolved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 935-940: 'This motivates the following colourful analogue.' then the conjecture is stated for Dirac graphs and trees, with no proof.

title: Proof of the strong conjecture about $F$-irregular graphs in the class of graphs $\{F\}$ of diameter $2$

interestingness_rationale: Natural combinatorial existence question with several nontrivial partial results (complete graphs, stars, biconnected min-degree-2, and diameter-2 solved here); a full resolution would be meaningful to specialists but appears technically specialized rather than foundational.
viability_rationale: 2024 result establishes infinitely many F-irregular graphs for all biconnected F with min degree ≥2; complete graphs and stars known. Remaining cases (cut-vertices, leaves) are technical but approachable, so moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Presented as a central, unresolved problem in the introduction and earlier literature; partial results exist but general existence remains open.

title: Structure and Spectrum of Nonergodic Nilsystems
body_tex:
\label{Conj}
   Let $(X=G/\Gamma,\mu,T_1,\ldots,T_d)$ be an ergodic $\Z^d$-nilsystem. Then the Koopman representation of $(T_1,\ldots,T_d)$ on $L^2(X,\mu)$ decomposes as
    \[
L^2(X,\mu)=E_{\mathrm{ds}}\oplus E_{\mathrm{Leb}},
\]
where $E_{\mathrm{ds}}\cong L^2(G/G_2\Gamma)$ has discrete spectrum and $E_{\mathrm{Leb}}$, if nontrivial, splits into a countable orthogonal sum of invariant subspaces, each having maximal spectral type absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure of a nontrivial subtorus of $\bS^d$.

title: The $G$-Noncommutative Minimal Model Program
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:1}
Let $\pi:X\to Y$ be a $G$-contraction of a smooth projective variety $X$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(A)] One can associate to $\pi$ a canonical family of quasi-convergent paths $\{\sigma_t^{\pi,\psi}\}$ in $\Stab(X_G)/\mathbb C$. Generic values of the parameter $\psi$ yield a $G$-linear semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$, and different generic values give mutation-equivalent decompositions.
    \item[(B)] For a generic $\psi$, the semiorthogonal factors of $D^b_G(X)$ are closed under tensor product with complexes of the form $\pi^{\ast}(E)$ for $E\in\mathrm{Perf}_G(Y)$.
    \item[(C)] Given a further $G$-contraction $Y\to Y'$, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ associated with $X\to Y'$ refines the decomposition associated with $X\to Y$.
    \item[(D)] If $\pi:X\to X'$ is a $G$-equivariant morphism of smooth varieties with $R\pi_{\ast}(\cO_X)=\cO_{X'}$, then for suitable parameters, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ asscoiated to $X\to Y$ refines the decomposition obtained by combining the semiorthogonal decomposition of 
    \[\pi^{\ast}(D^b_G(X'))\cong D^b_G(X')\]
    associated to $X'\to Y$ with the decomposition 
    \[
    D^b_G(X)=\langle\mathrm{ker}(\pi_{\ast}),\pi^{\ast}(D^b_G(X'))\rangle.
    \]
\end{itemize}

title: No-dimensional results of combinatorial convexity. Dimension strikes back
body_tex:
\label{conj:deterministic_rudelson}
Let $A_1,\dots,A_n\in \mathcal{M}_d$ be positive semidefinite and suppose that
\[
\lambda_1A_1+\cdots+\lambda_nA_n=\id_d,
\]
where the coefficients $\lambda_i$ are nonnegative and summing up to one.
Then for every $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ there exists a deterministic algorithm that selects indices
$i_1,\dots,i_k\in[n]$ with $k\le C(\varepsilon)\,d\ln d$ and outputs positive coefficients $\beta_1,\dots,\beta_k$
such that
\[
\Bigl\|\beta_1A_{i_1}+\cdots+\beta_kA_{i_k}-\id_d\Bigr\|_{S_\infty}\le \varepsilon.
\]

title: Monodromy rank and the semisimple Mumford-Tate conjecture for hyper-Kähler varieties
body_tex:
\label{conj:Mumford--Tate conjecture}
Fix a field embedding $K \subseteq \CC$. Let $\overline{K}$ be the algebraic closure of $K$ inside $\CC$. For any prime $\ell$, the isomorphism $\GL(\rH^{i}_{\et}(X_{\overline{K}},\QQ_{\ell})) \allowbreak\cong \GL(\rH^{i}_{B}(X))_{\QQ_{\ell}}$ via the Artin comparison isomorphism gives an identification
	\[
	\bG_{\ell,i}(X)^{\circ} =\MT_{i}(X_{\CC})_{\QQ_{\ell}}\,.
	\]
	Here $\bG_{\ell,i}(X)$ is the \emph{$\ell$-adic algebraic monodromy group} of $\rH^i_{\et}(X_{\overline{K}},\QQ_{\ell})$; and $\MT_i(X_{\CC})$ is the \emph{Mumford--Tate group} of the Hodge structure on the Betti cohomology $\rH^i_B(X,\QQ)$.

For any prime $$, the isomorphism $(^ i _ (X_ K ,_ )) (^ i _ B (X))_ _ $ via the Artin comparison isomorphism gives an identification \[ _ ,i (X)^ =_ i (X_ )_ _ \,. \] Here $_ ,i (X)$ is the $$-adic algebraic monodromy group of $^i_ (X_ K ,_ )$; and $_i(X_ )$ is the Mumford--Tate group of the Hodge structure on the Betti cohomology $^i_B(X,)$.

title: Remarks on the inverse Littlewood conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj-1}
Let $A\subset \bbz$ be a finite set of size $N$. If $\anorm{1_A}_1\leq K\log N$ then for some $m,r\ll_K 1$ the following holds. There are finite arithmetic progressions $P_1,\ldots,P_m\subset \mathbb{Z}$, each of length at most $N$, and finite sets $X_1,\ldots,X_r\subset \bbz$ such that $\big| \bigcup_{j}X_j\big|=o_K(N)$,
together with $\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_m,\eta_1,\ldots,\eta_r\in \{-1,1\}$ such that
\[1_A=\sum_{1\leq i\leq m}\epsilon_i 1_{P_i}+\sum_{1\leq j\leq r}\eta_j 1_{X_j}.\]

There are finite arithmetic progressions $P_1,,P_m Z$, each of length at most $N$, and finite sets $X_1,,X_r $ such that $| _ j X_j|=o_K(N)$, together with $_1,,_m,_1,,_r \ -1,1\ $ such that \[1_A=_ 1 i m _i 1_ P_i +_ 1 j r _j 1_ X_j .\]

title: Fano varieties with split tangent sheaf
body_tex:
\cite{Bea98}
\label{conjecturebeauville}
Let $X$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold such that $T_X = V_1 \oplus V_2$, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are {\em integrable} subbundles.
Let \holom{\mu}{\tilde{X}}{X} be the universal covering of $X$. Then there is a decomposition
$$
\tilde{X} \simeq X_1 \times X_2
$$
and the decomposition $T_X=V_1 \oplus V_2$ lifts to the canonical decomposition
$p_{X_1}^* T_{X_1} \oplus p_{X_2}^* T_{X_2}$.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold such that $T_X = V_1 V_2$, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are integrable subbundles. Let X X be the universal covering of $X$. Then there is a decomposition $$ X X_1 X_2 $$ and the decomposition $T_X=V_1 V_2$ lifts to the canonical decomposition $p_ X_1 ^* T_ X_1 p_ X_2 ^* T_ X_2 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Well-known Beauville conjecture: an integrable splitting of T_X should force a product decomposition of the universal cover. Central to classification of Kähler/Fano manifolds; widely studied with deep links to foliations and rationally connectedness.
viability_rationale: Well-known Beauville conjecture (1998). Strong partial results and Fano case solved, but integrability/rationally-connected cases remain open and widely studied. Classic, decades-old problem—low near-term resolution likelihood.
rationale: Beauville's splitting conjecture is cited as a well-known open conjecture about Kähler manifolds and is presented as unresolved in general.

title: Matching conditions for scattering solutions of scalar wave equations on extremal Reissner-Nordström spacetimes
body_tex:
\label{intro:conj:dhr}
		For the Einstein vacuum equations, given smooth scattering data settling down to a (possibly extremal) Kerr solution on $\hor$ and $\scri$ at a fast enough inverse polynomial decay rate, there exists a vacuum spacetime $(M, g)$ ``bounded by” $\hor$ and $\scri$, attaining the data, and that is regular away from $\hor$. However, for generic such data converging to a subextremal Kerr solution, the Christoffel symbols of the resulting metric fail to be locally square integrable near the horizon.

However, for generic such data converging to a subextremal Kerr solution, the Christoffel symbols of the resulting metric fail to be locally square integrable near the horizon.

title: The Carathéodory metric on Teichmüller space of genus two surface

interestingness_rationale: Central classification problem in Teichmüller theory linking metric equivalence, holomorphic retracts, and zero-order of quadratic differentials; resolved only in low-complexity cases (Tei_{0,5}, Tei_{2,0}), likely deep and consequential if fully solved.
viability_rationale: Conjecture proved in low-complexity cases (Tei_{0,5} and Tei_{0,6}/genus2) using Markovic's criterion. Progress suggests extension possible, but combinatorial/topological complexity for general (g,n) makes full resolution in five years uncertain.
rationale: Folklore/Gekhtman–Markovic conjecture stated explicitly; authors confirm it for genus two but it is posed as the general open classification problem.
evidence_snippet: Lines 151–156: "Gekhtman and Markovic made the following conjecture... A Teichmüller disk is a holomorphic retract iff generated by a quadratic differential with only even-order zeros." Lines 158–166: authors prove genus-two case.

Assume that the generic fiber of the restricted morphism $\phi|_{D} \colon D \to \phi(D)$ is either a cycle of $\PP^1$'s or a nodal elliptic curve. Then the generating series of logarithmic Gromov–Witten invariants of $(Y,D)$, 
\[ \sum_{d \geq 0} GW^{(Y,D)}_{g,v,\beta_0+dF} q^d \,, \]
where $v$ is a fixed contact order along $D$, $\beta_0 \in H_2(Y,\ZZ)$ is a fixed curve class, and $F$ denotes the fiber class, is a component of a (higher depth) vector-valued mock modular form, up to appropriate normalization in the power of $q$.

title: Ekedahl-Oort Types and Newton Polygons of Abelian Covers of $\mathbf{P}^1$ Branched at Three Points
body_tex:
\label{unlikely-density}
    Let $g \geq 18$, and let $m$ be the least common multiple of the exponents of all of the Galois groups of abelian covers of $\mathbf{P}^1$ branched at three points of genus $g$. If $p$ is a prime such that $p \not \equiv 1$ modulo $m$, then there exist abelian covers $X_1$ and $X_2$ of $\mathbf{P}^1$ branched at three points defined over $\overline{\mathbf{F}_p}$ such that $X_1$ has an unlikely Newton polygon and $X_2$ has an unlikely Ekedahl-Oort type.

rationale: A stronger conjecture is stated — every cycle-conformal brace is complete bipartite — presented as more general and explicitly conjectural, with authors expressing lower confidence.

title: Constructing Koszul filtrations: existence and non-existence for G-quadratic algebras
body_tex:

Let $G$ be a reduced Gröbner basis of quadratic binomials each having terms of disjoint support. Then $S/(G)$ has a Koszul filtration. Moreover, if $(G)$ is toric then $S/(G)$ has a monomial Koszul filtration.
plain_text: Let $G$ be a reduced Gröbner basis of quadratic binomials each having terms of disjoint support. Then $S/(G)$ has a Koszul filtration. Moreover, if $(G)$ is toric then $S/(G)$ has a monomial Koszul filtration.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects Gröbner combinatorics, quadratic binomial ideals, and Koszul filtrations (monomial in toric case); a positive resolution would impact Koszul and toric algebra theory, but seems tractable via combinatorial/Gröbner methods.
viability_rationale: Authors provide constructive examples and algorithms, and the hypothesis restricts to quadratic binomial Gröbner bases with disjoint supports (toric case monomial). This focused structure makes a resolution plausible within five years, but general obstructions are unknown.

title: Isomorphism of cosymplectomorphism groups implies diffeomorphism of manifolds
body_tex:
Let $(M_1, g_1, J_1, \omega_1)$ and $(M_2, g_2, J_2, \omega_2)$ be compact Kähler manifolds. Suppose their groups of Kähler isometries (holomorphic isometries) are isomorphic as Lie groups via
		\[
		\Phi: \IsomKah(M_1) \;\stackrel{\text{Lie}}\cong\; \IsomKah(M_2),
		\]
		and assume both groups have positive dimension (i.e., are non-discrete). Then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biholomorphic. Moreover, if the isomorphism $\Phi$ is induced by an algebraic correspondence between the groups of isometries, then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric up to a constant scaling factor of the Kähler metrics.
plain_text: Let $(M_1, g_1, J_1, _1)$ and $(M_2, g_2, J_2, _2)$ be compact Kähler manifolds. Suppose their groups of Kähler isometries (holomorphic isometries) are isomorphic as Lie groups via \[ : (M_1) \; Lie \; (M_2), \] and assume both groups have positive dimension (i.e., are non-discrete). Then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biholomorphic. Moreover, if the isomorphism $$ is induced by an algebraic correspondence between the groups of isometries, then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric up to a constant scaling factor of the Kähler metrics.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects finite-dimensional Kähler isometry groups to biholomorphic rigidity; a deep, nontrivial link between symmetry and complex structure, tied to classification of homogeneous Kähler manifolds and contrasts with discrete counterexamples.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the statement for coKähler manifolds; the general cosymplectic/Kähler extensions remain open and delicate (false without extra hypotheses). Strong special-case progress increases plausibility but nontrivial obstacles remain.
rationale: Paper formulates Kähler rigidity as a conjecture and explicitly states the full conjecture remains open (with caveats about counterexamples without extra hypotheses).
evidence_snippet: Lines 266–272,276–277: "Conjecture... If IsomKah(M1) ≃ IsomKah(M2) (positive-dim), then M1 and M2 are biholomorphic... The full conjecture remains open and is false without additional structure."

title: The Ising magnetisation field and the Gaussian free field
body_tex:

title: The Ising magnetisation field and the Gaussian free field
body_tex:
\label{conj:AT-inter}
	Consider the clusters of the decomposition described in Conjecture \ref{conj:AT-IGMC} under $+/+$ boundary conditions. Toss a fair coin for each cluster to assign a value $+1$ or $-1$, except for the boundary cluster which is assigned $+1$. If two clusters with the value $+1$ (resp. $-1$) touch each other, we say that they belong to the same $\oplus$-cluster (resp. $\ominus$-cluster). Then, the law of the outermost interfaces of the Ashkin-Teller magnetisation field is given by the outermost outer boundaries of the $\ominus$-clusters. The full collection of interfaces is defined iteratively.

Then, the law of the outermost interfaces of the Ashkin-Teller magnetisation field is given by the outermost outer boundaries of the $$-clusters. title: Stability of natural bundles on curves
body_tex:
\label{conj-main}
Let $f\colon X \to Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
plain_text: Let $f X Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Beauville's conjecture is a central open statement on stability of pushforwards for every cover; it affects moduli of bundles and theta linear series and is only settled for general Hurwitz covers, indicating deep geometric significance.
viability_rationale: The generic-case theorem covers all Hurwitz components, so strong partial progress exists. But Beauville's original statement for every fixed cover remains open and the base-locus is poorly understood, leaving substantial work.

title: Vigemers: on the number of $k$-mers sharing the same XOR-based minimizer
body_tex:
Let $g : \Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m$ be such that $g(a_1\cdots a_m) = g_1(a_1)\cdots g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma$. Then, by defining the minimizer $\min_g(x)$ of a \kmer $x$ as its leftmost \mmer $w$ that minimizes $\texttt{rank}(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute
$\pi_k^g(w) = \left\lbrace x\in\Sigma^k : \min_g(x) = w\right\rbrace$
in $O(|\Sigma|\cdot km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.

viability_rationale: Conjecture implies infinitely many primes p with ω(p−1)≤2 — a deep, long-standing structural prime problem. Only congruence-type restrictions are presented; no major breakthrough evidence, so low near-term resolution probability.

title: Irreducible distinguishing colourings and the Axiom of Choice
body_tex:
The following statements are equivalent the Axiom of Choice in ZF assuming the definition of a cardinal number as  an equivalence class of the equinumerous relation.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textnormal{\arabic*)}]
    \item Every infinite connected graph has a chromatic index.
    \item Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic number.
    \item Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic index.
    \item Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing number.
    \item Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic index.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: The following statements are equivalent the Axiom of Choice in ZF assuming the definition of a cardinal number as an equivalence class of the equinumerous relation. enumerate[label=)] Every infinite connected graph has a chromatic index. Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic number. Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic index. Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing number. Every infinite connected graph has a distinguishing chromatic index. enumerate
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalizes known Konig’s Lemma equivalences for locally finite graphs to all infinite connected graphs; proving equivalence to the Axiom of Choice would be a significant set-theoretic/combinatorial result linking fundamental graph-coloring invariants to AC.
viability_rationale: Recent equivalence results for locally finite graphs and a new irreducible-colouring reduction theorem provide concrete tools toward encoding choice; the problem is recent and targeted, giving moderate near-term solvability likelihood.
rationale: Explicitly stated as an open conjecture relating several graph-colouring statements to the Axiom of Choice in ZF; authors note the question remains open to their knowledge.

title: Erdős--Turán Theorem and Eulerian Integers
body_tex:

If $f\in\mathbb Z[x,y]$ is a two-variable polynomial that is not decomposable as $f(x,y)=g(x)h(y)$,

$\mc A,\ \mc B\subseteq\mathbb Z^+$ are finite sets and
$|\mc{A}|\geq|\mc{B}|\geq 2$, then
\[
\omega_{\mathbb N}(\prod_{a\in{\mc A},b\in{\mc B}}(f(a,b)))\geq{c\log|\mc A|}.
\]

title: Stability of natural bundles on curves
plain_text: Let $X$ be a general rational curve of degree $e$ in $G(k, n)$, where without loss of generality we take \(k n-k\). Then $N_ X/G(k, n) $ is balanced if and only if either \(e = 1\) or none of the following occur: itemize The degeneracy exceptions: We have \(e < n-k\) and \(e k\). The characteristic \(2\) exceptions: The characteristic is $2$ and \(k = 1\) and \(e 1\) mod \(n-k - 1\). The tangent bundle splitting exceptions: We have $q_1+ q_2 (k-r_1)(n-k-r_2)$ and \(r_1 r_2 0\). itemize
label: real_open_conjecture

Solving it would clarify bundle splitting behavior and have broad impact.
viability_rationale: Partial progress: N_{X/G} proven 2-balanced; conjecture settled for (k,n)=(2,4),(2,5),(2,6) in char 0. Wide range of (k,n,e) remains; moderate but limited near-term likelihood.

title: On the Graphical $r$-Stirling Numbers of the First Kind for Specific Graph Families
body_tex:
\label{conj:unimodal-planar}
For any planar graph $G$ of order $n$, the sequence of graphical Stirling numbers
\[
\{\st{G}{k}\}_{k=1}^n
\]
is \emph{unimodal}, i.e., there exists an index $k_0$ such that
\[
\st{G}{1} \le \cdots \le \st{G}{k_0} \ge \cdots \ge \st{G}{n}.
\]
This property is known for paths, cycles, trees, lollipop, and Tadpole graphs.
plain_text: For any planar graph $G$ of order $n$, the sequence of graphical Stirling numbers \[ \ G k \ _ k=1 ^n \] is unimodal, i.e., there exists an index $k_0$ such that \[ G 1 G k_0 G n . \] This property is known for paths, cycles, trees, lollipop, and Tadpole graphs.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extending unimodality from paths, cycles, trees, lollipop and Tadpole to all planar graphs would impose strong structural constraints on graphical Stirling numbers, linking to real-rootedness/log-concavity and impacting algebraic combinatorics and graph enumeration.
viability_rationale: Authors prove many special cases (paths, cycles, trees, lollipop, tadpole), indicating progress, but the general planar/chordal/r‑restricted statements are broad and nontrivial; moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: Explicitly posed conjecture in the paper: unimodality of graphical Stirling numbers for all planar graphs; supported by known cases but unproven generally.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For any planar graph G of order n, the sequence { {G\brack k} }_{k=1}^n is unimodal (there exists k_0 with increasing then decreasing).

\label{conj:real-rooted}
If $G$ is a chordal graph, then the graphical cycle polynomial of the first kind
\[
\mathcal{C}(G, x) = \sum_{k=1}^n \st{G}{k} x^k
\]
has only real roots. Real-rootedness would imply log-concavity and unimodality of the sequence $\{\st{G}{k}\}$.

viability_rationale: Observed true for several families (stars, paths); combinatorial/injection or generating-function methods seem plausible for wider classes, but universal "for any graph" claim and limited current progress keep likelihood moderate.
rationale: Paper explicitly conjectures real-rootedness of the graphical cycle polynomial for chordal graphs; presented as an open problem implying log-concavity/unimodality.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If G is a chordal graph, then C(G,x)=∑_{k=1}^n {G\brack k} x^k has only real roots. Real-rootedness would imply log-concavity and unimodality.

title: The Gromov-Hausdorff distance between $l^p$-products of metric spaces
body_tex:
Suppose that for arbitrary sequences of metric spaces
$\{(X_n, d_{X_n}\}_{n \in \N}$, \hfill\break $\{(Y_n, d_{Y_n}\}_{n \in \N}$,
$\sup_{n \in \N} \diam(X_n) < \infty$ and $\sup_{n \in \N} \diam(Y_n) < \infty$.
Then is the estimate

\]

viability_rationale: Unimodality for all planar graphs is broad; only shown for few families (paths, cycles, trees). Chordal real-rootedness is conjectured but unproven—decisive resolution within five years seems unlikely.
rationale: Explicitly stated open conjecture in the paper asserting monotonicity of r-restricted graphical Stirling numbers as r increases; no proof provided.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For any graph G and fixed k, the r-restricted numbers satisfy {G\brack k}_1 ≥ {G\brack k}_2 ≥ … ≥ {G\brack k}_k, i.e., they decrease as restricted set size increases.

title: Base change fundamental lemma for Bernstein centers of principal series blocks

interestingness_rationale: General local Langlands for p-adic reductive groups is a central, deep conjecture; resolving it would majorly impact representation theory, trace formulas and the Bernstein center. Many cases are known, but full generality remains open.
viability_rationale: Specialized modern problem with foundational tools (LLC+, stable Bernstein center, known fundamental lemmas) making progress plausible, but snippet shows no strong recent breakthroughs or reductions; five-year resolution uncertain.
rationale: The general Local Langlands Correspondence (LLC) is presented as the standard conjectural statement; it remains open in full generality and is posed here as a conjecture.

title: Cohomological rigidity of solvable Lie algebras of maximal ran
body_tex:
Let $p$ denotes the number of weights $\Lambda$ for which the difference between the maximal number of pairwise distinct triples and the maximal number of distinct pairs associated with $\Lambda$ greater than $1$. Then
\[
H^{2}(\mathcal{R}_{\mathcal{T}},\mathcal{R}_{\mathcal{T}}) \;\geq\; p.
\]
plain_text: Let $p$ denotes the number of weights $$ for which the difference between the maximal number of pairwise distinct triples and the maximal number of distinct pairs associated with $$ greater than $1$. Then \[ H^ 2 (R_ T ,R_ T ) \;\; p. \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects explicit weight/root combinatorics to a lower bound on H^2, giving tangible criteria for non-rigidity; meaningful for classification of solvable maximal-rank algebras though specialized to a narrow family.
viability_rationale: Specialized, recent work with several explicit examples and a concrete combinatorial criterion; narrow scope and available cohomological techniques make a proof or counterexample plausible within a few years, but a general proof is not yet indicated.

title: Topology of projective Tate-Shafarevich twists
body_tex:
\label{saccaconj}
    Total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations that are related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent.
plain_text: Total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations that are related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Concerns deformation classification of irreducible hyper-Kähler varieties; proving it would show Tate–Shafarevich twists do not produce new deformation types and tie Hodge/Beauville–Bogomolov invariants to global geometry.
viability_rationale: Author proves Hodge-isomorphism generally and establishes Saccà's conjecture under concrete hypotheses using Bogomolov–Kamenova–Verbitsky; significant partial progress suggests plausible near-term resolution, but general case still nontrivial.
rationale: This is Saccà's conjecture from the literature; the paper discusses and proves versions under hypotheses, but the general conjecture remains an actively considered open question.
evidence_snippet: Lines 68–70 and 86–90: "\begin{conjecture} Total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent. \end{conjecture}. Theorem: under assumptions we have H^k(X)≅H^k(X^φ) as Hodge structures."

title: Topology of projective Tate-Shafarevich twists
body_tex:
\label{intro:saccaconjorig}
    The relative compactified Albanese of a Lagrangian fibration with integral fibers stays in the same deformation class.
plain_text: The relative compactified Albanese of a Lagrangian fibration with integral fibers stays in the same deformation class.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Determines whether Tate-Shafarevich twists can produce new deformation types of irreducible hyper-Kähler (Lagrangian) fibrations; crucial for classification and moduli. Cohomological evidence exists, but proving global deformation equivalence is delicate and deep.
viability_rationale: H^k and Q-Hodge-structure equality for projective Tate–Shafarevich twists is proved (Saccà/Ngô and current work), giving strong evidence; but deformation-equivalence is a strictly stronger geometric statement with no general reconstruction known.
rationale: The original Saccà conjecture (relative compactified Albanese stays in same deformation class) is presented as a conjecture; the paper proves cases but the general statement remains part of the literature's open questions.
evidence_snippet: Lines 73–77: "In [sacca] she ... constructed the relative compactified Albanese ... From there, she conjectured: \begin{conjecture} The relative compactified Albanese ... stays in the same deformation class. \end{conjecture}"

title: New bounds for (weak) sequenceability in $\mathbb{Z}_k$
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
Let $p$ be a prime. Then every subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_p \setminus\{0\}$ has a valid ordering.
plain_text: Let $p$ be a prime. Then every subset $A Z_p \ 0\ $ has a valid ordering.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classical Graham conjecture in additive combinatorics; recently proved for sufficiently large primes but full generality (all primes and general abelian groups) remains open. A complete resolution would be broadly significant to specialists.
viability_rationale: Recent breakthrough proving the conjecture for all sufficiently large primes, plus improved rectification/probabilistic bounds, suggest remaining finite/small-prime cases are tractable — plausible full resolution within five years.
rationale: Graham's conjecture (valid orderings in Z_p) is presented as a conjecture; partially resolved for sufficiently large primes, but not stated as fully settled here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 129–134 and 152: statement of Graham's conjecture and note that Pham–Sauermann proved it for sufficiently large primes p.
